text: The surgeon carefully maintains a preperitoneal approach during the robotic left direct inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon performs medial dissection down to the pubic tubercle, colon, and Cooper's ligament as part of the TEP and TAP inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection to prevent tension on the flap and avoid inadvertent tearing of the peritoneum.
The surgeon identifies a large direct hernia sac within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins to reduce the large direct hernia sac, carefully manipulating the tissue to reposition it within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon observes that the tissue usually separates easily during the dissection process.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection using a blunt technique, carefully separating tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgical field shows no presence of an indirect hernia sac during the procedure.
The surgeon performs further inferior dissection below the obturator space, revealing a typical fat plug in the area.
The surgeon performs final lateral dissection to create space for the placement of a 12x16 ProGrip mesh, ensuring adequate inferior coverage for the direct hernia repair.

The surgeon identifies a lipoma hidden beneath wispy transverse fibers and carefully clears the area to reveal it. The lipoma is then resected, ensuring that the testicle is not inadvertently reduced during the procedure.
The surgeon examines the final dissection area and the placement of the mesh prosthesis overlay (MPO) within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs further lateral dissection to create additional space for mesh placement.
The surgeon performs an invocation of the pseudosec, which involves manipulating the attenuated transversal fascia, typically done for larger direct hernia defects.
The surgeon centers the mesh slightly more laterally than usual, adjusting its position for a large indirect defect during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon uses a remnant suture to imbricate the direct hernia sac, securing it in place.
The surgeon fixates the mesh medially to secure it and prevent early migration, addressing the larger direct defects in the area of weakness.
The surgeon proceeds to close the peritoneal flap, ensuring the mesh is securely positioned beneath the peritoneum.
The surgeon repeatedly secures the mesh in place, ensuring it is firmly anchored to prevent movement.
The surgeon uses an angiocath to evacuate gas from the peritoneal flap, ensuring that the mesh is positioned flat against the tissue.
